system_prompt:
You are distilling one chapter of a guitar method book into a short summary.

The user will give you a chapter file with topic-organized verbatim quotes
plus the human-rationale notes for each. Write a one-paragraph summary (4-7
sentences) that captures:

  - what this chapter teaches (the techniques, concepts, rules, examples)
  - the master's characteristic moves visible in this chapter (if any)
  - the load-bearing terminology this chapter introduces

Reference the topic labels from the chapter file when useful. Do not invent
information not present in the chapter. If the chapter is biographical or
introductory rather than instructional, say so explicitly.

Output just the paragraph — no preamble, no JSON, no markdown formatting.
user_prompt:
Chapter 10: Section Three/Part 2: Self Accompanying

<<<CHAPTER FILE
# Chapter 10 — Section Three/Part 2: Self Accompanying

_Pages 121-130._

> **Verbatim-quote constraint.** The quotes below are exact substrings of the raw transcript produced by `pdftotext`. The transcript may contain extraction artifacts where the book's typeface confuses the tool — e.g. `7irst` or `<irst` for `first`, `speci7ic` for `specific`. **Do NOT manually fix these in this file**: the quote-fidelity validator compares against the raw transcript, so a 'fixed' quote will fail verification and break Stage 2 idempotency. If a future pipeline iteration adds artifact-normalization, it must normalize the transcript and these files together.

## Mixed harmonic movement

> It is most common to find mixed harmonic movement. This example starts on a | chord and
> moves to a V chord which sets it up for a cycle move to Cmaj7 in the second measure. Notice that
> the lowest note of Gmaj7 in the first measure is D, the fifth, and the lowest note of G7(5) is b5.
> This was done to continue the effect of chromatic movement from Gmaj7 to G7(5) to Cmaj7. The
> harmony then moves by half step to the finish.

**Significance (p. 120).** Voice-leading through bass-note choice for chromatic continuity.

## Ascending half-step harmony

> In many cases the diminished seventh chord is the in-between, the hamburger helper of ascend-
> ing harmonic movement.

**Significance (p. 120).** Diminished seventh as standard connector chord for ascending sequences.

## Root movement bass

> The bass notes of the first two measures are the same as the chord names above.

**Significance (p. 121).** Simplest self-accompaniment baseline.

## Doubled bass notes

> These measures have doubled bass notes, a style used by musicians playing upright bass in the
> swing era of jazz. Otherwise, the movement of harmony is the same as the first example above.

**Significance (p. 121).** Doubled-bass stylistic device anchored in swing-era practice.

## Root/fifth alternation

> In this self accompanying style the bass alternates between the root and the fifth of each chord.
> Root/fifth is often associated with simple music, such as polka bands, country music and the like.
> Root/fifth is used in jazz too, but usually not in such a rigid oom-pah fashion.

**Significance (p. 122).** Defines root/fifth alternation with stylistic warning.

## Other alternating bass notes

> Keeping a strict pattern of root/seventh is not as important as long as the effect
> (low to high alternation) is there.

**Significance (p. 123).** Effect matters more than strict interval adherence.

## Walking bass chromatic

> One of the most common bass note moves is approaching notes — especially
>
> indicated. In bar one the chromatic passing

**Significance (p. 124).** Chromatic approach-note rule defining jazz walking bass.

## Anticipation bass device

> Leading with a bass note is a way to give variety to accompaniment instead of just chugging
> along with four bass notes to a measure.

**Significance (p. 124).** Anticipation as device to break four-on-the-floor monotony.

## Delaying bass line

> The opposite of leading with the lower line is trailing or delaying it. This too steers away from
> heaviness.

**Significance (p. 124).** Delay as paired counterpart to anticipation.

## Arpeggiation melody emphasis

> For slower moving tunes, arpeggiation is a natural for fingerstyle playing. Don't forget to empha-
> size the melody note so that it won't get lost in the sea of eighth note soup.

**Significance (p. 125).** Arpeggiation appropriate for slow tunes, melody projection required.

## Two-note accompaniment sufficiency

> A melody accompanied by two additional notes is often all you need to make an arrangement
> sound full, whether you are playing by yourself or with a group.

**Significance (p. 125).** Sparse voicing produces full-sounding arrangement.

## Two-note inner voices technique

> You can pluck the two inner voices with i and m, or strike them both with p.

**Significance (p. 126).** Right-hand fingering options for two-note inner-voice work.

## Melody/bass overlap solution

> Often the melody may merge with and become the top part of the two note bass accompaniment.
>
> It's not a problem, it’s a solution.

**Significance (p. 126).** Reframes voice-leading collision as valid arrangement choice.

## Moving tenths swing piano device

> Moving tenths in the left hand was one of the primary accompanying devices of pianists in the
> swing era. Fats Waller, Willie “The Lion” Smith, Art Tatum, Teddy Wilson, and many others made
> this the style until bebop came along. Within reason, you can emulate it on the guitar. The pianists
> mentioned often kept almost constant quarter-note moving tenths going with the melody. You
> should be able to manage at least half notes.

**Significance (p. 127).** Defines moving-tenths device with historical source and adaptation target.

## Closer voicing fourths and tritones

> This variation uses the same chords except the accompaniment is voiced so that the harmony
> moves in alternating pairs of fourths and tritones. The bottom note in each pair alternates between
> major or minor seventh to major third. There is no explicitly stated root or fifth of the chord. If you play
> in a group, the bassist will cover roots and fifths.

**Significance (p. 129).** Rootless close-voicing system using fourths and tritones.

CHAPTER FILE>>>
